channel.title: Flathead Valley Real Estate Insights | Ryan Berner
channel.description: Market updates, buyer and seller guides, and straight answers for Kalispell and Whitefish real estate — including divorce and estate home sales. By Ryan Berner, West and Company | Brokered by eXp Realty.

item.title: Inherited a Home in the Flathead Valley? Start Here
item.description: A plain-English walkthrough of how estate home sales actually work in Kalispell and the Flathead Valley — what's urgent, what can wait, and where families lose money by rushing.
item.content:encoded:
Inherited a Home in the Flathead Valley? Start Here

If you've recently inherited a home in Kalispell, Whitefish, or anywhere in the Flathead Valley, here's the question I hear most often: "What am I actually supposed to do first?"

It's usually asked by someone who is grieving, coordinating with siblings in three different time zones, and staring at a house full of forty years of memories. And here's what most people get wrong: they assume everything is urgent. It isn't. A few things genuinely are — and knowing the difference is where families either protect the estate's value or quietly lose a piece of it.

Let's walk through it in plain English.

First: understand what probate actually is (and isn't)

Probate is simply the court-supervised process of settling an estate — validating the will, appointing a personal representative, paying debts, and distributing what's left. In Montana, most estates go through what's called informal probate, which is far less dramatic than the word "probate" sounds.

Two things matter for the house specifically. One: in most cases, nobody can legally sell the property until the court appoints a personal representative (sometimes called an executor) with authority to act. Two: Montana probate typically runs a minimum of several months from start to finish, and often longer when real property is involved.

I'm a real estate agent, not an attorney, so treat this as orientation, not legal advice — a Montana probate attorney should confirm how the process applies to your specific estate. But the practical takeaway is this: the sale of the home usually happens inside a longer legal timeline, which means you have more breathing room than you think.

What's actually urgent in the first 30 days

Not the sale. Not the price. Not the flooring. Here's what is:

Secure and insure the property. A vacant home in the Flathead Valley is exposed — frozen pipes in winter, unnoticed roof leaks, and the simple reality that empty houses attract problems. Call the insurance carrier and tell them the home is unoccupied; many homeowner policies restrict or lapse coverage on vacant properties, and an uncovered loss can take a six-figure bite out of the estate.

Keep utilities on. Heat in winter is non-negotiable here. A burst pipe in January can do more damage in a weekend than a bad sale price ever would.

Locate the documents. The will, the deed, any mortgage statements, property tax records, and insurance policies. The personal representative will need all of it.

Do not start giving things away. Until the personal representative is appointed and the estate's obligations are clear, the contents of the home are estate property. Well-meaning family members emptying the garage in week one creates conflict — and sometimes legal problems — later.

What can wait (and usually should)

Everything cosmetic. Families under stress often pour money into renovations an inherited home doesn't need, or swing the other way and dump the property as-is at a discount because "we just want this done."

Both cost real money. The right move is a clear-eyed assessment: what does this specific home, in this specific neighborhood, actually need to sell well? Sometimes that's a deep clean, a dumpster, and fresh landscaping. Rarely is it a $60,000 kitchen. An agent who knows the Kalispell and Whitefish markets can walk the property and tell you where the line is — before you spend a dollar.

The same goes for clearing out the home. It has to happen, but it doesn't have to happen this month. Estate sale companies, donation pickups, and family distribution all work better with a plan than with a panic.

Where families lose money by rushing

After years of working with estates and other high-stakes sales, the pattern is consistent. Families lose money in three places:

Selling before the market can respond. Accepting an early, unsolicited cash offer because it feels simple. Those offers exist because they're profitable — for the buyer. A properly prepared and marketed home in the Flathead Valley almost always nets the estate meaningfully more, even after the extra weeks.

Skipping the tax conversation. Inherited property generally receives a step-up in basis — meaning capital gains are typically calculated from the value at the date of death, not what your parents paid in 1987. That can dramatically reduce the tax owed on a sale. But the details matter and situations vary, so before you sell, talk to a CPA. This is orientation, not tax advice.

Letting distance drive the decision. Many heirs I work with live out of state. Managing showings, contractors, and paperwork from Denver or Seattle feels impossible, so they take the path of least resistance. It doesn't have to work that way. With the right local team — agent, attorney, cleanout crew — an out-of-state personal representative can run the entire sale remotely with weekly updates and zero surprises.

A calm process beats a fast one

My background is military and government protective services, and if that work taught me anything, it's this: pressure is exactly when you slow down and follow the plan. An estate sale is a mission with phases — secure the asset, establish legal authority, prepare, market, close. Handled in order, it's manageable. Handled in a panic, it's expensive.

So here's the question worth sitting with: if you inherited a Flathead Valley home tomorrow, would you know which decisions actually need to be made this month — and which ones can wait until the estate is ready?

If you're in that situation now, or see it coming, you don't have to figure out the sequence alone. Email me at ryan@westcompanies.com and I'll walk you through how the process works for your specific situation — confidential, no pressure, no obligation.

Ryan Berner is a real estate agent with West and Company, brokered by eXp Realty, serving Kalispell, Whitefish, and the greater Flathead Valley. A US Army veteran and former government protective-services officer, Ryan brings calm, disciplined execution to every transaction — especially when the circumstances are complicated. Have a question about your situation? Email Ryan directly at ryan@westcompanies.com for a confidential, no-pressure conversation.

item.title: Kalispell & Whitefish Market Update — July 2026
item.description: Current Flathead Valley home prices, inventory, and mortgage rates — and what they actually mean if you're buying or selling in Kalispell or Whitefish right now.
item.content:encoded:
If you've been watching the Flathead Valley market and wondering whether now is a smart time to make a move, here's the honest answer: it depends on which side of the transaction you're on — and most people are reading the headlines wrong.

Let's walk through the actual numbers, then talk about what they mean for you.

The Numbers Right Now

Here's where the Kalispell market stands as of the June 2026 data (source: Movoto/MLS, June 2026):

  • Median list price in Kalispell: $627,495
  • Price per square foot: $339
  • Active listings: 732 — that's real selection for buyers
  • Median days on market: 58, compared to 55 a year ago

Zoom out to the broader picture: Zillow puts the typical Kalispell home value at about $562,000 as of late May 2026, essentially flat — up just 0.2% over the past year. And for context, across greater Flathead County in 2025, single-family homes posted a median price of $689,000, with 1,238 sales — more transactions than the year before.

On the financing side, the 30-year fixed mortgage averaged 6.55% for the week of July 16, 2026, per Freddie Mac.

What This Actually Means

Here's the question worth asking yourself: what does a flat market with growing inventory actually reward?

It rewards preparation. Let me break that down for both sides.

If You're Buying in Kalispell or Whitefish

With 732 active listings and homes taking nearly two months to go under contract, you have something buyers haven't had much of in the last five years: leverage and time.

Flat prices mean you're not chasing a runaway train. Sellers who've been sitting for 60+ days are negotiable — on price, on closing costs, on rate buydowns. A 1-point permanent buydown on a $560,000 purchase can matter more to your monthly payment than a $15,000 price cut. Most buyers never ask. The prepared ones do.

And if you're a veteran or active-duty service member: your VA loan benefit is one of the strongest financing tools in this market — zero down, no PMI, competitive rates. As an Army veteran myself, I walk fellow veterans through this one all the time, and it consistently surprises people how much buying power it unlocks here in the valley.

If You're Selling

Read those days-on-market numbers again: 58 days median. The listings that sell fast in this market share three traits — they're priced to the data (not to what a neighbor got in 2022), they're prepared before they hit the MLS, and they're marketed with a plan rather than a prayer.

Overpricing in a flat market isn't ambitious. It's expensive. The market tells you within two weeks whether you missed, and chasing the price down afterward almost always nets less than pricing right on day one.

My approach comes from a background where planning wasn't optional: we brief the mission before we execute. Pricing strategy, prep checklist, marketing timeline, negotiation contingencies — mapped out before your sign goes in the yard.

If You're Selling Under Complicated Circumstances

One more thing, because it doesn't get said enough: a meaningful share of home sales in any market happen because life changed — a divorce, a death in the family, an estate to settle. If that's your situation, the market data above still applies to you, but the process looks different. Timelines may be set by a court. Two parties may need to sign off on every decision. Discretion matters.

That's a lane I work in deliberately, with a process built for it. If that's where you are, you don't need a salesperson — you need a steady hand and a clear plan.

The Bottom Line

The Flathead Valley isn't crashing and it isn't booming. It's normalizing — and normal markets reward the people who move with a plan instead of a hunch.

So here's the question to sit with: if the right property — or the right buyer — showed up in the next 30 days, would you be ready?

If you're not sure, that's worth a conversation — email me directly at ryan@westcompanies.com.

Market data sources: Movoto/MLS Kalispell market trends (June 2026), Zillow Home Value Index (May 2026), Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Market Survey (July 16, 2026). This article is for informational purposes only and is not financial, legal, or tax advice.

Ryan Berner is a real estate agent with West and Company, brokered by eXp Realty, serving Kalispell, Whitefish, and the greater Flathead Valley. A US Army veteran and former government protective-services officer, Ryan brings calm, disciplined execution to every transaction — especially when the circumstances are complicated. Have a question about your situation? Email Ryan directly at ryan@westcompanies.com for a confidential, no-pressure conversation.
